title: 25 Tir — St. Tekle Haymanot & The Assembly
narrative:
title": "25 Tir — St. Tekle Haymanot & The Assembly

narrative": "The twenty-fifth day of Tir bears the monthly commemoration of Saint Tekle Haymanot, the great Ethiopian monastic father whose name means 'Plant of the Holy Trinity' — a name that in the light of Tir takes on its fullest theological resonance, for it is the Baptism of the Lord at the Jordan that reveals the Holy Trinity in its fullness, and the one who is planted in that Trinitarian mystery is the one whose life most fully manifests the fruit of the Epiphany. Tekle Haymanot's extraordinary ascesis — the years of standing prayer, the withered leg, the solitude of Debre Libanos — is the most radical expression in Ethiopian hagiography of what Gregory of Nyssa called epektasis: the soul's ceaseless stretching toward the God who is always further and deeper and greater than any experience of Him. The 'Plant of the Holy Trinity' does not merely receive the Epiphany light at Timqat; he spends his entire life growing toward it, rooted in the water of his baptism, reaching toward the opened heaven.

Saint Gregory the Theologian, the Patriarch of Constantinople whose eloquent defense of the Trinitarian faith earned him the unique title 'the Theologian' in the Eastern tradition, is commemorated on this day. Gregory — whose name means 'watchful' or 'awake' — preached a series of five theological orations in Constantinople that remain the summit of patristic Trinitarian theology. His meditation on the Baptism of the Lord, in his great oration On the Holy Lights, describes the Epiphany as the illumination of the world by the three-fold light of the Trinity: the Light from the Father, refracted through the Son who stands in the water, radiated outward by the Spirit who descends upon Him. 'Three Suns,' Gregory writes, 'but one Light' — and the feast of Timqat is the feast of that one Light shining in three modes upon the face of the water and transforming it from an element of nature into a sacrament of the new creation.

Saint Mares the Hermit, a Syrian ascetic of the fourth or fifth century venerated in the Syrian and Ethiopian traditions, completes this day's assembly. Mares — whose name in the Syriac tradition carries a resonance with the word for 'lord' or 'master' — was a solitary who dwelt in a remote region of Syria in conditions of great austerity, and who was renowned for the gift of tears. The gift of tears — compunctio, the piercing of the heart by a grief that is simultaneously joy, a weeping for sin that is also the weeping of the lover for the Beloved — is the interior counterpart of the water of Timqat: as the blessed water flows outward in the public feast, the tears of the hesychast flow inward in the hidden feast of the cell, and both are the same water, the water that the Lord hallowed at the Jordan by His descent.

The theological seal of this twenty-fifth day of Tir is the mystery of growth toward the Trinitarian light. Tekle Haymanot is the plant rooted in the Trinity and growing ceaselessly toward its fullness; Gregory the Theologian is the lamp that illuminates the Trinitarian mystery in the language of human thought; Mares the Hermit waters the root with the silent tears of interior compunction. All three together depict the full life of the soul that has truly received the gift of Timqat: planted in the water, illuminated by the Voice, watered by the tears of its own longing, growing silently toward the opened heaven from which the Father has spoken the word that gives it its true name.
